# Change as Primary Risk

## The Physics

Most production failures are caused by changes, not by latent defects. The
correlation between deployment activity and incident rate is one of the most
robust findings in systems engineering. A system that has not been changed is
not safe — it is accumulating entropy (see JOS-C21) — but the act of changing
it is the moment of highest risk.

This is because a change introduces a delta between a known state and an
unknown state. The known state has been validated by production traffic.
The unknown state has been validated only by testing, which is necessarily a
subset of production conditions. Every change is a bet that the test
environment is a sufficient model of production. Some percentage of those
bets lose.

The risk is proportional to the size of the change, the breadth of the blast
radius, and the reversibility of the deployment. A small change to a single
component with an instant rollback path is a small bet. A coordinated change
across multiple services with no rollback path is a large one. The physics
does not say "don't change" — systems that don't change die of entropy. It
says: make small bets, verify quickly, and ensure every bet is reversible.

## Why This Matters

- **Deployment strategy**: Small, incremental deployments reduce per-change risk even if they increase deployment frequency.
- **Change tracking**: Every production change must be traceable because change is where incidents originate.
- **Verification**: The rigor of pre-deployment verification should be proportional to the blast radius of the change.
- **Rollback**: A change without a rollback plan is a one-way door — and one-way doors should be rare and deliberate.

## Observable Patterns

- Incident timelines overwhelmingly begin with "a change was deployed at..."
- Deployment freezes reduce incidents in the short term but increase change size — and therefore risk — when the freeze lifts.
- Teams that deploy more frequently with smaller changes have lower incident rates than teams that deploy infrequently with large changes.
- The most dangerous changes are those that modify shared infrastructure, because their blast radius spans multiple consumers.

## 1970/2070 Note

A 1970 operator patching a running mainframe knew that the patch itself was
the most likely cause of the next outage. A 2070 engineer pushing a firmware
update to a quantum processor will face the same calculus. The technology
changes; the risk profile of change does not.

## Relationship to Meta Layer

- JOS-P13 (Small Reversible Changes) — the direct architectural response to change-as-risk
- JOS-P18 (Verification Proportional to Risk) — verification intensity follows blast radius
- JOS-R07 (No Untraced Production Changes) — traceability exists because change is the primary failure vector

### Framework Connection

Framework §14 (versioning model) operationalizes change-as-risk through blast radius reasoning with three dimensions — Direction, Reach, and Reversibility — giving every change a structured risk profile.
